title: Upper GI Bleed — Variceal
oneLiner: Life-threatening haematemesis/melaena from ruptured oesophageal/gastric varices in cirrhosis/portal hypertension; requires immediate resuscitation, vasoactive drugs, antibiotics, and emergency endoscopy.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Exsanguination (uncontrolled variceal haemorrhage)**
  - Single finding: Bright red haematemesis with haemodynamic instability (SBP <90 mmHg, HR >120) not correcting after 2 L crystalloid.
- **Aspiration pneumonitis / airway obstruction**
  - Single finding: Vomiting large-volume blood with stridor, gurgling, or oxygen saturations <90% on high-flow O₂.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy / sepsis (spontaneous bacterial peritonitis or aspiration)**
  - Single finding: Altered consciousness (GCS <13) or fever >38°C with neutrophilia rising within hours of bleed.
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - Haematemesis: fresh red blood (not coffee-ground) — massive, recurrent.
  - Melaena: black, tarry, foul-smelling stool (may be absent in acute massive bleed).
  - Signs of chronic liver disease: ascites, spider naevi, palmar erythema, jaundice, splenomegaly.
- Key exam findings:
  - Tachycardia, hypotension, oliguria.
  - Stigmata of portal hypertension: caput medusae, abdominal wall collaterals.
  - Asterixis (liver flap) if encephalopathic.
  - Rectal exam: melaena (if not already passed).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- Most likely -> less likely:
  - **Peptic ulcer bleed:** slower onset, “coffee-ground” emesis, epigastric pain, NSAID use; no signs of CLD.
  - **Mallory-Weiss tear:** history of retching/alcohol binge, self-limiting, less massive.
  - **Gastritis/erosive oesophagitis:** milder, no portal hypertension signs.
  - **Malignancy (e.g. gastric/varices secondary to portal vein thrombosis):** weight loss, abdominal mass, thrombocytosis.
- **Splitter:** Body habitus + stigmata of chronic liver disease → variceal bleeding (even without known cirrhosis); absence of those signs → likely non-variceal source.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - ABC assessment, full set of vital signs (including SpO₂, NEWS2).
  - Secure two large-bore IV cannulae (14–16G) and draw blood simultaneously.
  - ECG if elderly or arrhythmia suspected.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, coagulation screen (PT/INR, APTT), group & save (cross-match 4–6 units).
  - Lactate, venous blood gas (pH, base deficit).
  - Consider: ethanol level, ammonia (non-urgent but prognostic if >150 µmol/L).
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: None acutely; proceed to endoscopy.
  - Second-line: CT angiogram (triple-phase) if endoscopy fails or in haemodynamically unstable patient unable to tolerate OGD — identifies portal vein thrombosis, TIPS candidate.
- **Special tests:**
  - Upper GI endoscopy (OGD) — diagnostic and therapeutic; aim within 12 hours of suspected variceal source.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Urgent OGD** — delays increase mortality; even in unstable patient, consider after initial resuscitation (do not wait for normal vitals).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Airway protection: left lateral position, suction, consider intubation if GCS <8 or active haematemesis.
  - Resuscitation: target Hb 70–80 g/L (avoid over-transfusion — raises portal pressure). Give packed red cells only if Hb <70 or ongoing haemorrhage.
  - Start terlipressin 2 mg IV bolus → then 1–2 mg IV every 4–6 hours (do NOT wait for endoscopy).
  - Give prophylactic antibiotics: ceftriaxone 1 g IV once daily (or piperacillin-tazobactam if penicillin-allergic; check local policy).
  - Do NOT offer tranexamic acid (NICE: no benefit in variceal bleed).
  - Consider metoclopramide 10 mg IV (or erythromycin 250 mg IV) to clear stomach for endoscopy (local policy dependent).
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - Continue terlipressin for up to 5 days.
  - Continue antibiotics for 5–7 days (ceftriaxone 1 g IV OD).
  - Endoscopic band ligation (EBL) performed at index OGD; if gastric varices, consider glue injection.
  - Monitor for rebleeding (haemodynamic changes, drop in Hb, fresh haematemesis).
  - Start secondary prophylaxis: after initial control, refer to hepatology for beta-blocker (propranolol or carvedilol) + EBL every 2–4 weeks until varices eradicated.
  - If refractory bleeding despite medical + endoscopic therapy → balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore tube) → ICU step-down; if ongoing, discuss TIPS.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Eradication of varices with banding programme.
  - Continue non-selective beta-blocker indefinitely.
  - Monitor for hepatocellular carcinoma (6-monthly ultrasound).
  - Consider liver transplantation decompensated cirrhosis.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - NBM until OGD performed (if OGD is immediate, then clear liquids post-procedure).
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride or balanced crystalloid (avoid dextrose unless hypoglycaemic). Rate: 500 mL–1 L over 4–6 h unless shocked (then faster). Monitor urine output.
  - VTE prophylaxis: withhold once variceal patient until after OGD and stable (high bleed risk). Reassess 24–48 h if no active bleeding (but caution with anticoagulants).
  - Observations: NEWS2 every 1 hour initially, then every 4 hours when stable for 12 h. Strict fluid balance (goal: 1 mL/kg/h urine output).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Terlipressin (first-line vasoactive agent)**
  - Dose: Initial 2 mg IV bolus → then 1–2 mg IV every 4–6 hours for up to 5 days.
  - Contraindications: severe hypotension (relative), pregnancy, severe peripheral vascular disease.
  - Monitor for ischaemic complications (abdominal pain, peripheral ischemia, hypertension).
  - *Most dangerous error:* Omitting terlipressin because blood pressure is low (it can actually raise BP; give slow bolus over 1–2 min). Over-transfusion while on terlipressin increases portal pressure → rebleed.
- **Ceftriaxone (antibiotic prophylaxis)**
  - Dose: 1 g IV once daily for 5–7 days.
  - Contraindication: severe penicillin allergy (use piperacillin-tazobactam or levofloxacin [check local policy]).
  - *Most dangerous error:* Not giving it — omitting antibiotics triples the risk of sepsis and mortality.
- **Non-selective beta-blockers (secondary prophylaxis, long-term)**
  - Propranolol: start 40 mg PO BD, titrate to resting HR 55–60 bpm (max 320 mg daily). Carvedilol: 6.25–12.5 mg PO OD.
  - Contraindications: asthma, heart block, acute decompensated cirrhosis (use with caution).
  - *Most dangerous error:* Starting in acute active bleed (must wait 24–48 h after haemostasis).
- **Alternative:** Octreotide 50 mcg IV bolus then 50 mcg/h infusion (less evidence than terlipressin; used if terlipressin unavailable. [Check local policy]).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Hypovolaemic shock / multi-organ failure.
  - Aspiration pneumonia / chemical pneumonitis.
  - Rebleeding (occurs in 30–50% within 48 h without effective management).
  - Hepatic encephalopathy.
  - Acute kidney injury (hepatorenal syndrome type 1).
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent variceal haemorrhage (without prophylaxis).
  - Decompensation of cirrhosis (ascites, jaundice, encephalopathy).
  - Hepatocellular carcinoma.
sections.Prognosis: - 6-week mortality for first variceal bleed: ~15–20% (higher if Child-Pugh C or active bleeding at endoscopy). Rebleeding risk within 1 year ~40% without secondary prophylaxis; with banding + beta-blocker, reduces to ~15%.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Start terlipressin and antibiotics** as soon as variceal bleed is suspected — do not wait for haematology or endoscopist.
- **Endoscopy within 12 hours** — earlier is better; if active bleeding at OGD, request banding, not sclerotherapy.
- **Check ammonia level** and consider lactulose/enemas if encephalopathy develops early.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Over-transfusion** — target Hb 70–80 g/L; higher Hb raises portal pressure and worsens bleeding.
- **Omitting antibiotics** — increases mortality from sepsis; always give ceftriaxone (or equivalent) for 5–7 days.
- **Starting anticoagulation or antiplatelet therapy** in the acute phase — risk of fatal rebleed.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** any fresh haematemesis, black stool with dizziness, severe epigastric pain, or confusion (encephalopathy).
- **Follow-up:** outpatient hepatology clinic within 2 weeks; schedule banding session for variceal eradication; continue beta-blocker; avoid alcohol and NSAIDs.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **NEWS2 >5:** inform medical registrar immediately.
- **Persistent hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg)** despite 2 L crystalloid → call critical care outreach / ICU.
- **Active haematemesis after initial control** → re-call endoscopy team; consider balloon tamponade if waiting.
- **GCS drop by >2 points** → possible hepatic encephalopathy; discuss with hepatology registrar.
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L or pH <7.2** → escalation to ICU for vasopressors and monitoring.

rawMarkdown:
## Upper GI Bleed — Variceal
**One-liner:** Life-threatening haematemesis/melaena from ruptured oesophageal/gastric varices in cirrhosis/portal hypertension; requires immediate resuscitation, vasoactive drugs, antibiotics, and emergency endoscopy.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Exsanguination (uncontrolled variceal haemorrhage)**
  - Single finding: Bright red haematemesis with haemodynamic instability (SBP <90 mmHg, HR >120) not correcting after 2 L crystalloid.
- **Aspiration pneumonitis / airway obstruction**
  - Single finding: Vomiting large-volume blood with stridor, gurgling, or oxygen saturations <90% on high-flow O₂.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy / sepsis (spontaneous bacterial peritonitis or aspiration)**
  - Single finding: Altered consciousness (GCS <13) or fever >38°C with neutrophilia rising within hours of bleed.

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - Haematemesis: fresh red blood (not coffee-ground) — massive, recurrent.
  - Melaena: black, tarry, foul-smelling stool (may be absent in acute massive bleed).
  - Signs of chronic liver disease: ascites, spider naevi, palmar erythema, jaundice, splenomegaly.
- Key exam findings:
  - Tachycardia, hypotension, oliguria.
  - Stigmata of portal hypertension: caput medusae, abdominal wall collaterals.
  - Asterixis (liver flap) if encephalopathic.
  - Rectal exam: melaena (if not already passed).

### Differential Diagnosis
- Most likely -> less likely:
  - **Peptic ulcer bleed:** slower onset, “coffee-ground” emesis, epigastric pain, NSAID use; no signs of CLD.
  - **Mallory-Weiss tear:** history of retching/alcohol binge, self-limiting, less massive.
  - **Gastritis/erosive oesophagitis:** milder, no portal hypertension signs.
  - **Malignancy (e.g. gastric/varices secondary to portal vein thrombosis):** weight loss, abdominal mass, thrombocytosis.
- **Splitter:** Body habitus + stigmata of chronic liver disease → variceal bleeding (even without known cirrhosis); absence of those signs → likely non-variceal source.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - ABC assessment, full set of vital signs (including SpO₂, NEWS2).
  - Secure two large-bore IV cannulae (14–16G) and draw blood simultaneously.
  - ECG if elderly or arrhythmia suspected.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, coagulation screen (PT/INR, APTT), group & save (cross-match 4–6 units).
  - Lactate, venous blood gas (pH, base deficit).
  - Consider: ethanol level, ammonia (non-urgent but prognostic if >150 µmol/L).
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: None acutely; proceed to endoscopy.
  - Second-line: CT angiogram (triple-phase) if endoscopy fails or in haemodynamically unstable patient unable to tolerate OGD — identifies portal vein thrombosis, TIPS candidate.
- **Special tests:**
  - Upper GI endoscopy (OGD) — diagnostic and therapeutic; aim within 12 hours of suspected variceal source.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Urgent OGD** — delays increase mortality; even in unstable patient, consider after initial resuscitation (do not wait for normal vitals).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Airway protection: left lateral position, suction, consider intubation if GCS <8 or active haematemesis.
  - Resuscitation: target Hb 70–80 g/L (avoid over-transfusion — raises portal pressure). Give packed red cells only if Hb <70 or ongoing haemorrhage.
  - Start terlipressin 2 mg IV bolus → then 1–2 mg IV every 4–6 hours (do NOT wait for endoscopy).
  - Give prophylactic antibiotics: ceftriaxone 1 g IV once daily (or piperacillin-tazobactam if penicillin-allergic; check local policy).
  - Do NOT offer tranexamic acid (NICE: no benefit in variceal bleed).
  - Consider metoclopramide 10 mg IV (or erythromycin 250 mg IV) to clear stomach for endoscopy (local policy dependent).
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - Continue terlipressin for up to 5 days.
  - Continue antibiotics for 5–7 days (ceftriaxone 1 g IV OD).
  - Endoscopic band ligation (EBL) performed at index OGD; if gastric varices, consider glue injection.
  - Monitor for rebleeding (haemodynamic changes, drop in Hb, fresh haematemesis).
  - Start secondary prophylaxis: after initial control, refer to hepatology for beta-blocker (propranolol or carvedilol) + EBL every 2–4 weeks until varices eradicated.
  - If refractory bleeding despite medical + endoscopic therapy → balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore tube) → ICU step-down; if ongoing, discuss TIPS.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Eradication of varices with banding programme.
  - Continue non-selective beta-blocker indefinitely.
  - Monitor for hepatocellular carcinoma (6-monthly ultrasound).
  - Consider liver transplantation decompensated cirrhosis.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - NBM until OGD performed (if OGD is immediate, then clear liquids post-procedure).
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride or balanced crystalloid (avoid dextrose unless hypoglycaemic). Rate: 500 mL–1 L over 4–6 h unless shocked (then faster). Monitor urine output.
  - VTE prophylaxis: withhold once variceal patient until after OGD and stable (high bleed risk). Reassess 24–48 h if no active bleeding (but caution with anticoagulants).
  - Observations: NEWS2 every 1 hour initially, then every 4 hours when stable for 12 h. Strict fluid balance (goal: 1 mL/kg/h urine output).

### Drugs & Doses
- **Terlipressin (first-line vasoactive agent)**
  - Dose: Initial 2 mg IV bolus → then 1–2 mg IV every 4–6 hours for up to 5 days.
  - Contraindications: severe hypotension (relative), pregnancy, severe peripheral vascular disease.
  - Monitor for ischaemic complications (abdominal pain, peripheral ischemia, hypertension).
  - *Most dangerous error:* Omitting terlipressin because blood pressure is low (it can actually raise BP; give slow bolus over 1–2 min). Over-transfusion while on terlipressin increases portal pressure → rebleed.
- **Ceftriaxone (antibiotic prophylaxis)**
  - Dose: 1 g IV once daily for 5–7 days.
  - Contraindication: severe penicillin allergy (use piperacillin-tazobactam or levofloxacin [check local policy]).
  - *Most dangerous error:* Not giving it — omitting antibiotics triples the risk of sepsis and mortality.
- **Non-selective beta-blockers (secondary prophylaxis, long-term)**
  - Propranolol: start 40 mg PO BD, titrate to resting HR 55–60 bpm (max 320 mg daily). Carvedilol: 6.25–12.5 mg PO OD.
  - Contraindications: asthma, heart block, acute decompensated cirrhosis (use with caution).
  - *Most dangerous error:* Starting in acute active bleed (must wait 24–48 h after haemostasis).
- **Alternative:** Octreotide 50 mcg IV bolus then 50 mcg/h infusion (less evidence than terlipressin; used if terlipressin unavailable. [Check local policy]).

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Hypovolaemic shock / multi-organ failure.
  - Aspiration pneumonia / chemical pneumonitis.
  - Rebleeding (occurs in 30–50% within 48 h without effective management).
  - Hepatic encephalopathy.
  - Acute kidney injury (hepatorenal syndrome type 1).
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent variceal haemorrhage (without prophylaxis).
  - Decompensation of cirrhosis (ascites, jaundice, encephalopathy).
  - Hepatocellular carcinoma.

### Prognosis
- 6-week mortality for first variceal bleed: ~15–20% (higher if Child-Pugh C or active bleeding at endoscopy). Rebleeding risk within 1 year ~40% without secondary prophylaxis; with banding + beta-blocker, reduces to ~15%.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Start terlipressin and antibiotics** as soon as variceal bleed is suspected — do not wait for haematology or endoscopist.
- **Endoscopy within 12 hours** — earlier is better; if active bleeding at OGD, request banding, not sclerotherapy.
- **Check ammonia level** and consider lactulose/enemas if encephalopathy develops early.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Over-transfusion** — target Hb 70–80 g/L; higher Hb raises portal pressure and worsens bleeding.
- **Omitting antibiotics** — increases mortality from sepsis; always give ceftriaxone (or equivalent) for 5–7 days.
- **Starting anticoagulation or antiplatelet therapy** in the acute phase — risk of fatal rebleed.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** any fresh haematemesis, black stool with dizziness, severe epigastric pain, or confusion (encephalopathy).
- **Follow-up:** outpatient hepatology clinic within 2 weeks; schedule banding session for variceal eradication; continue beta-blocker; avoid alcohol and NSAIDs.

### Escalation Criteria
- **NEWS2 >5:** inform medical registrar immediately.
- **Persistent hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg)** despite 2 L crystalloid → call critical care outreach / ICU.
- **Active haematemesis after initial control** → re-call endoscopy team; consider balloon tamponade if waiting.
- **GCS drop by >2 points** → possible hepatic encephalopathy; discuss with hepatology registrar.
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L or pH <7.2** → escalation to ICU for vasopressors and monitoring.
